---
title: "3.3 Anatomy of a Class"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Anatomy of a Java Class

### Topic intro

In Unit 1, we learned to use **classes** and **objects** that are built-in to Java or written by other programmers.

In this unit, you will learn to write your own classes and make your own objects.

## Class and Instance

### Blueprint and objects

Remember that a **class** in Java defines a blueprint for creating objects.

When you create **objects**, you create new **instances** (实例) of that class.

What you can do with those instances is determined by what methods are defined in the class.

## Unit 1 Review

### Objects created from classes

For example, we created `yertle` and `myrtle`, two `Turtle` variables, and assigned them references to objects created from the class `Turtle`.

We also used instances of Java's `String` class to assign values to different `String` variables.

## Parts of a Class

### Class definition and body

Most classes you write will have the keyword `public` before them, though it is not required.

The class definition itself always starts with the word `class` followed by the name of the class.

The rest of the class, called the **body** (类体), is defined inside a pair of `{}`.

## Person Skeleton

### Basic class shape

Since we're talking about anatomy, let's create a class called `Person`.

```java
public class Person
{
    // define class here - also called the "body" of the class

}
```

Classes are almost always named with capitalized names, though this is a matter of style, not a rule of the language.

## Creating Person Objects

### Declaration and construction

You can create instances of the `Person` class with `new` as in `new Person()`.

You can declare variables that can hold a reference to a `Person` object with `Person variableName`.

```java
Person ada = new Person();
Person charles = new Person();
```

## Class Body

### Attributes and behaviors

So what makes up the body of the class, the stuff between the `{}`?

Remember that objects have both **attributes** and **behaviors**.

These correspond to **instance variables** (实例变量) and **methods** (方法) in the class definition.

## Instance Variables

### Each instance has its own data

The first things we define in a class are usually the instance variables.

They are called that because each instance of the class, each object, has its own set of variables that are not shared with other instances.

This is what allowed `yertle` and `myrtle` to be at different positions at the same time.

## Constructors

### Initialize instance variables

The next thing we define in a class is usually its **constructors** (构造器).

We will talk about writing constructors in more detail in the next section.

A constructor's job is to initialize the instance variables when the object is created.

In this course, constructors are always designated `public`.

## Methods

### Behaviors of objects

The real meat of a class is in the **methods**, which define the behaviors of the objects of that class.

Most methods either do things, like the `Turtle` methods that moved the turtle on the screen, or return values like `getXPos` and `getYPos` on `Turtle`.

## Method Calls Use Object Data

### Same method, different object

The methods of the class share access to the object's instance variables.

When a method is called on an object, it uses the instance variables for that object.

For example, in the `Turtle` class, the `forward` method changes an instance variable `xPos`.

## Three Main Features

### Class anatomy

Putting it all together, the three main anatomical features of a class are:

::: {.tight-list}
- **instance variables**, which hold values associated with each object
- **constructors**, whose job is to initialize the instance variables
- **methods**, which contain the code that gives the objects their behavior
:::

## Person Class Layout

### Skeleton with roles

```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables

    // constructors

    // methods

}
```

## The Main Method

### Testing or program entry point

Any Java class can have a `main` method.

The `main` method can be used to run that class as a program:

::: {.tight-list}
- to test that one class
- sometimes as the entry point to a whole program made up of many classes and objects
:::

## Designing a Class

### What data does it represent?

Now that we know what the skeleton of a class looks like and the elements that make up the body of the class, we are ready to create our own class.

One important question we have to ask when designing a class is:

**What data does it represent?**

## Person Design

### Data depends on the problem

In one program, perhaps an address book, we might want to know the person's name, phone number, and email.

In another program, such as a medical application, we might need to know their vital signs such as blood pressure, temperature, and pulse rate.

For now, let's go with the address book example.

## Active Code

### `activecode:: PersonClass`

The following `Person` class keeps track of the name, email, and phone number of a person.

Try changing the `Person p2` object in the `main` method to your name.

Use the code as a trace example: execution always starts in the `main` method.

## PersonClass Full Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phoneNumber;

    // constructor: construct a Person copying in the data into the instance
    // variables
    public Person(String initName, String initEmail, String initPhone)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
        phoneNumber = initPhone;
    }

    // Print all the data for a person
    public void print()
    {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
        System.out.println("Email: " + email);
        System.out.println("Phone Number: " + phoneNumber);
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // call the constructor to create a new person
        Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com", "123-456-7890");
        // call p1's print method
        p1.print();
        Person p2 = new Person("Jean", "jean@gmail.com", "404 899-9955");
        p2.print();
    }
}
```
:::

## Trace Focus

### `main`, constructor, and `print`

When `p1.print()` is called, the code defined in the `print` method runs.

When `print` gets the values of `name`, `email`, and `phoneNumber`, it gets the specific values set by the constructor for that object.

Repeat the trace for `p2.print()`.

## Expected Output

### Runestone test target

```text
Name: Sana
Email: sana@gmail.com
Phone Number: 123-456-7890
Name: Jean
Email: jean@gmail.com
Phone Number: 404 899-9955
```

The Runestone test checks the expected output from `main`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define a class as a blueprint for creating objects
- explain that objects are instances of a class
- identify the class body inside `{}` braces
- name the three main class features: instance variables, constructors, and methods
- explain that a method call uses the instance variables for the object it is called on
- trace that execution starts in `main`
:::

## End

### 3.3 Part 1 complete

Next: data encapsulation and instance variables.
